title: Prime factorization of a number
description: Decompose N into prime factors with exponents

approaches.name: Brute Force O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers from 2 to sqrt(N), dividing N by each prime factor found and counting exponents. This is straightforward and works for any N, but can be slow for large numbers with many small factors.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization with Exponents
ladders.desc: Given an integer N, return its prime factorization as a list of [prime, exponent] pairs.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires decomposing a number N into its prime factors along with their exponents. The brute force O(√N) approach works by iterating through potential divisors from 2 to √N. For each divisor i, if N is divisible by i, i must be prime (since any composite divisor would have been divided out earlier by its prime factors). We repeatedly divide N by i while counting the exponent. After the loop, if N > 1, it's a prime factor itself. The intuition: any composite number N has at least one prime factor ≤ √N (if both factors were > √N, their product would exceed N). By dividing out found factors, we reduce N, ensuring we only check up to the current √N. Time: O(√N) worst-case (when N is prime). Space: O(1) excluding output. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of repeatedly dividing by a factor. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle the leftover N > 1, or using i ≤ N instead of i*i ≤ N (which would be O(N)). Real code pattern: result = []; for i from 2 to sqrt(N): if N%i==0: count=0; while N%i==0: N//=i; count+=1; result.append([i,count]); if N>1: result.append([N,1]); return result.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Repeated Division by a Factor
ladders.desc: Given N and a divisor d, repeatedly divide N by d while counting how many times it divides exactly.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine extracts all occurrences of a prime factor d from N. The intuition: if d divides N, then N = d * k. By repeatedly dividing N by d while N % d == 0, we remove all powers of d from N, leaving a smaller number that is not divisible by d. This is efficient because each division reduces N significantly. Step-by-step: Initialize count=0. While N % d == 0: N = N // d; count += 1. Return (updated N, count). Time: O(log_d(N)) worst-case (when N is a power of d). Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This is the inner loop that handles each prime factor. Common mistakes: not updating N in place, or using floating-point division. Real code pattern: while N % d == 0: N //= d; count += 1; return N, count.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Up to √N and Divide Out Factors
ladders.desc: Given N, iterate i from 2 to √N, and whenever i divides N, process i as a prime factor.
ladders.explanation: This is the core loop of the factorization algorithm. The intuition: if N is composite, it has a prime factor p ≤ √N (because if all prime factors were > √N, their product would exceed N). By iterating i from 2 to √N, we check each potential prime factor. When we find an i that divides N, we know i is prime (since any composite i would have been divided out earlier by its smaller prime factors). We then use the L1 subroutine to divide out all powers of i. After the loop, if N > 1, it's a prime factor > √N. Step-by-step: Initialize result list. For i from 2 to √N: if N % i == 0: call L1 to get new N and count; append [i, count]. If N > 1: append [N, 1]. Time: O(√N) worst-case. Space: O(1) excluding output. Connection to L0: This is the main algorithm structure. Common mistakes: using i ≤ N instead of i*i ≤ N, or not updating N after division. Real code pattern: result = []; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(N))+1): if N%i==0: N, count = subroutine(N,i); result.append([i,count]); if N>1: result.append([N,1]); return result.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Divisibility and Update N
ladders.desc: Given N and a divisor d, check if d divides N, and if so, update N by dividing by d once.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two operations: checking divisibility (N % d == 0) and performing integer division (N = N // d). The intuition: divisibility tells us if d is a factor of N. If it is, dividing N by d removes one instance of that factor, reducing N. This is a fundamental step in factorization. Step-by-step: If N % d == 0: N = N // d; return (True, new N). Else: return (False, N). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the basic operation inside the core loop, extended in L1 to handle multiple divisions. Common mistakes: using floating-point division or not handling division by zero. Real code pattern: if N % d == 0: N //= d; return True, N; else: return False, N.

examples.trace: Start: N=15, d=3. 15 % 3 == 0? Yes. N = 15 // 3 = 5. Return True, N=5.
Start: N=14, d=3. 14 % 3 == 0? No (14 % 3 = 2). Return False, N=14.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo
ladders.desc: Given two integers a and b, compute a // b (integer division) and a % b (remainder).
ladders.explanation: These are fundamental arithmetic operations. Integer division a // b gives the quotient (how many times b fits into a completely), and modulo a % b gives the remainder. The intuition: for factorization, we use modulo to check divisibility (if a % b == 0, then b divides a), and integer division to reduce a when a factor is found. Step-by-step: Quotient = a // b (floor division). Remainder = a - (a // b) * b. Time: O(1) typically. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used to check and perform division. Common mistakes: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), or misunderstanding negative operands (but for factorization, we use positive numbers). Real code pattern: quotient = a // b; remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: a=15, b=5. Quotient: 15 // 5 = 3 (since 5*3=15). Remainder: 15 % 5 = 0 (since 15 - 5*3 = 0).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factors
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and what it means for a number to be a factor of another.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself (e.g., 2, 3, 5, 7). A composite number has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself (e.g., 4, 6, 8). A factor (or divisor) of a number N is an integer that divides N exactly, i.e., N % factor == 0. For example, factors of 12 are 1,2,3,4,6,12. Prime factorization expresses a number as a product of prime factors, e.g., 12 = 2^2 * 3. The intuition: every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This foundation is crucial because factorization aims to find these prime factors. Connection to L4: We use division to test for factors. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime (it's not), or confusing factors with multiples. Real code pattern: To check if p is prime: if p < 2: return False; for i from 2 to sqrt(p): if p%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: 6 is composite. Its factors: 1,2,3,6. Prime factors: 2 and 3 (since 2 and 3 are prime). 6 = 2 * 3.
11 is prime (only divisors: 1 and 11). Its prime factorization is just 11.

approaches.name: Sieve-Optimized O(sqrt(N)/log(sqrt(N)))
approaches.summary: Precompute primes up to sqrt(N) using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, then use only these primes for trial division. This reduces the number of divisions compared to brute force, especially for large N with many composite divisors.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(sqrt(N) log log sqrt(N) + sqrt(N)/log sqrt(N))

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization with Sieve-Optimized Trial Division
ladders.desc: Complete implementation: precompute primes up to sqrt(N) using Sieve of Eratosthenes, then factor N using only those primes.
ladders.explanation: This approach combines two classic algorithms for optimal prime factorization. The intuition is that any composite number N must have at least one prime factor ≤ sqrt(N). By precomputing all primes up to sqrt(N) using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, we eliminate the need to test composite numbers during trial division, dramatically reducing the number of divisions. The Sieve runs in O(sqrt(N) log log sqrt(N)) time and O(sqrt(N)) space. After obtaining the prime list, we iterate through primes ≤ sqrt(N), dividing N by each prime while it's divisible, counting exponents. Any remaining N > 1 after this process is itself prime (since all primes ≤ sqrt(N) have been tested). The total time is O(sqrt(N) log log sqrt(N) + sqrt(N)/log sqrt(N)), where the second term represents trial division using only primes (density ~1/log(sqrt(N))). This is significantly faster than brute force trial division (O(sqrt(N))) for large N. The space is O(sqrt(N)) for storing primes. Connection to L1: This level integrates the prime sieve (L1) with the factorization logic. Common mistakes include: not handling N=0 or 1 (should return empty), forgetting to check remaining N>1, or inefficient sieve implementation. Real code pattern: def prime_factorization(N): if N<=1: return []; limit=int(sqrt(N)); primes=sieve(limit); factors=[]; for p in primes: if p*p>N: break; count=0; while N%p==0: N//=p; count+=1; if count>0: factors.append([p,count]); if N>1: factors.append([N,1]); return factors.

examples.trace: Step 1: sqrt(84)=9.16→limit=9. Step 2: Sieve primes up to 9→[2,3,5,7]. Step 3: Initialize factors=[], N=84. Step 4: Test p=2: 84%2=0→count=1, N=42; 42%2=0→count=2, N=21; 21%2≠0→add [2,2]. Step 5: Test p=3: 21%3=0→count=1, N=7; 7%3≠0→add [3,1]. Step 6: Test p=5: 7%5≠0→skip. Step 7: Test p=7: 7%7=0→count=1, N=1; add [7,1]. Step 8: N=1≤1→done. Output: [[2,2],[3,1],[7,1]].

Step 1: sqrt(97)=9.85→limit=9. Step 2: Sieve primes up to 9→[2,3,5,7]. Step 3: Initialize factors=[], N=97. Step 4: Test p=2: 97%2≠0→skip. Step 5: Test p=3: 97%3≠0→skip. Step 6: Test p=5: 97%5≠0→skip. Step 7: Test p=7: 97%7≠0→skip. Step 8: After loop, N=97>1→add [97,1]. Output: [[97,1]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sieve of Eratosthenes for Prime Generation
ladders.desc: Generate all prime numbers up to a given limit using the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm for finding all primes up to a limit L. The intuition is to iteratively mark multiples of each prime as composite, starting from 2. By the time we process a number, if it hasn't been marked composite, it must be prime. This works because any composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root, so we only need to sieve up to sqrt(L). The algorithm: 1) Create boolean array is_prime[0..L] initialized to True (except 0,1=False). 2) For p from 2 to sqrt(L): if is_prime[p] is True, mark all multiples of p from p*p to L as False (starting at p*p because smaller multiples were already marked by smaller primes). 3) Collect all indices where is_prime[i] is True. Time complexity is O(L log log L) due to harmonic series of primes. Space is O(L). Connection to L0: This sieve provides the prime list needed for optimized trial division. Common mistakes: incorrect array bounds, starting marking from p instead of p*p (redundant work), or not handling 0 and 1. Real code pattern: def sieve(limit): if limit<2: return []; is_prime=[True]*(limit+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for p in range(2,int(sqrt(limit))+1): if is_prime[p]: for multiple in range(p*p,limit+1,p): is_prime[multiple]=False; return [i for i,prime in enumerate(is_prime) if prime].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_prime=[F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T] (indices 0-10). Step 2: sqrt(10)=3.16→p from 2 to 3. Step 3: p=2, is_prime[2]=True→mark multiples: 4,6,8,10→is_prime[4]=F, is_prime[6]=F, is_prime[8]=F, is_prime[10]=F. Step 4: p=3, is_prime[3]=True→mark multiples: 9→is_prime[9]=F. Step 5: Collect primes: indices 2,3,5,7 where True. Output: [2,3,5,7].

Step 1: Initialize is_prime array size 21. Step 2: sqrt(20)=4.47→p from 2 to 4. Step 3: p=2→mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20. Step 4: p=3→mark 9,15 (12,18 already marked). Step 5: p=4→is_prime[4]=False, skip. Step 6: Collect primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Output: [2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trial Division Using Only Primes
ladders.desc: Factor a number N by dividing it only by prime numbers up to sqrt(N), counting exponents.
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the factorization algorithm. The intuition relies on the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every integer >1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes. By testing only primes (not all numbers), we skip composite divisors, which cannot divide N if their prime factors don't divide N. The algorithm: given a list of primes up to sqrt(N), iterate through primes. For each prime p, while N is divisible by p, divide N by p and increment count. If count>0, record [p,count]. Stop early if p*p > N (no further prime factors ≤ sqrt(N) possible). After the loop, if N>1, it's a prime factor > sqrt(N). Time: O(sqrt(N)/log sqrt(N)) divisions (number of primes up to sqrt(N)). Space: O(1) excluding input. Connection to L0: This uses the prime list from L1 to perform efficient factorization. Common mistakes: not stopping when p*p>N (wasteful), forgetting to handle remaining N, or incorrect exponent counting. Real code pattern: def factor_using_primes(N, primes): factors=[]; for p in primes: if p*p>N: break; count=0; while N%p==0: N//=p; count+=1; if count>0: factors.append([p,count]); if N>1: factors.append([N,1]); return factors.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize factors=[], N=56. Step 2: p=2, p*p=4≤56→test: 56%2=0→count=1, N=28; 28%2=0→count=2, N=14; 14%2=0→count=3, N=7; 7%2≠0→add [2,3]. Step 3: p=3, p*p=9>7→break loop. Step 4: N=7>1→add [7,1]. Output: [[2,3],[7,1]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Square Root and Limit Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the integer square root of N to determine the upper bound for prime factors.
ladders.explanation: This concept is crucial because in prime factorization, we only need to test factors up to sqrt(N). The intuition: if N has a factor f > sqrt(N), then the complementary factor N/f must be < sqrt(N), so it would have been found earlier. Thus, checking up to sqrt(N) suffices. The integer square root (floor of sqrt(N)) gives the maximum possible prime factor to test. Algorithm: compute limit = floor(sqrt(N)). In code, use integer sqrt via math.isqrt(N) in Python or while loop. Time: O(log N) for built-in sqrt, O(sqrt(N)) for naive loop. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This limit determines which primes to generate and use in trial division. Common mistakes: using floating-point sqrt (precision issues), not taking floor, or miscalculating for large N. Real code pattern: def integer_sqrt(N): if N<0: raise error; return int(N**0.5) # or math.isqrt(N).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute sqrt(100)=10.0 exactly. Step 2: Floor is 10. Output: 10.
Step 1: Compute sqrt(101)≈10.049875. Step 2: Floor is 10. Output: 10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Division and Modulo for Factor Testing
ladders.desc: Check if a number is divisible by another using modulo operation, and perform integer division.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental arithmetic operation in factorization. The intuition: to test if prime p divides N, we check if N % p == 0 (modulo gives remainder). If divisible, we update N = N // p (integer division) to remove that factor. Repeating this counts the exponent. Algorithm: while N % p == 0: count += 1; N = N // p. Time: O(log_p N) divisions per prime. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied repeatedly within the trial division loop up to the sqrt limit. Common mistakes: using floating-point division, not using integer division, or infinite loops if not updating N. Real code pattern: while N % p == 0: N //= p; count += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize count=0, N=24. Step 2: 24%2=0→count=1, N=12. Step 3: 12%2=0→count=2, N=6. Step 4: 6%2=0→count=3, N=3. Step 5: 3%2≠0→stop. Output: count=3, new_N=3.
Step 1: count=0, N=35. Step 2: 35%5=0→count=1, N=7. Step 3: 7%5≠0→stop. Output: count=1, new_N=7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and the basic concept of divisibility.
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute foundation. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2,3,5,7,11. Composite numbers have additional divisors. Divisibility: a number a divides b (b % a == 0) if there exists integer k such that b = a*k. The intuition for factorization: any integer >1 can be broken down into primes uniquely. This is why we test divisibility by primes. Algorithm to check if n is prime: test divisors from 2 to sqrt(n). Time: O(sqrt(n)). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Divisibility testing is the core operation used in factor testing. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, or not understanding modulo. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2,int(sqrt(n))+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7>1. Step 2: sqrt(7)=2.65→test i=2: 7%2=1≠0. Step 3: i=3: 7%3=1≠0. Step 4: No divisors found→prime. Output: True.
Step 1: n=9>1. Step 2: sqrt(9)=3→test i=2: 9%2=1≠0. Step 3: i=3: 9%3=0→divisible→composite. Output: False.

approaches.name: Pollard's Rho O(N^(1/4))
approaches.summary: Use a probabilistic algorithm based on cycle detection and modular arithmetic to find factors quickly, especially effective for large semi-prime numbers. It combines with trial division for small factors and recursion for complete factorization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization Using Pollard's Rho Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prime factorization using Pollard's Rho algorithm with trial division and recursion.
ladders.explanation: This solution decomposes a number N into its prime factors with exponents using Pollard's Rho algorithm, which is particularly effective for large semi-prime numbers. The intuition behind Pollard's Rho is based on the birthday paradox and cycle detection in modular arithmetic. If we generate a pseudo-random sequence modulo N, any non-trivial factor d of N will cause collisions in the sequence modulo d faster than modulo N, allowing us to detect factors through gcd computations. The algorithm combines: 1) Trial division for small factors (up to N^(1/3)) to handle small primes efficiently, 2) Miller-Rabin primality test to avoid unnecessary factorization of primes, 3) Pollard's Rho with Floyd's cycle detection to find a single non-trivial factor, and 4) Recursion to factor the found factor and cofactor completely. The expected time complexity is O(N^(1/4)) due to the Rho algorithm's efficiency in finding factors, with space O(log N) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This level integrates all sub-routines from lower levels into a complete system. Common mistakes include: not handling negative numbers or 0/1 properly, forgetting to use Miller-Rabin to check primality before factorization (leading to infinite recursion), using poor polynomial functions in Rho (f(x)=x²+c works well with c≠0,-2), and not implementing trial division for small factors (which significantly improves practical performance). Real code pattern: def pollard_rho(n): if n%2==0: return 2; x=y=2; d=1; c=random.randint(1,n-1); f=lambda x:(x*x+c)%n; while d==1: x=f(x); y=f(f(y)); d=math.gcd(abs(x-y),n); return d if d!=n else None. Then recursively factor d and n//d.

examples.trace: Step 1: Trial division up to 100^(1/3)≈4.64, check 2: 100%2=0, factor=2, exponent=2 (100/2=50, 50/2=25), remaining=25. Check 3: 25%3≠0. Check 4: not prime, skip. Step 2: Miller-Rabin on 25: composite. Step 3: Pollard's Rho on 25: x=y=2, c=1, f(x)=x²+1 mod 25. Iteration 1: x=5, y=26 mod25=1, gcd(|5-1|,25)=gcd(4,25)=1. Iteration 2: x=26 mod25=1, y=f(f(1))=f(2)=5, gcd(|1-5|,25)=gcd(4,25)=1. Iteration 3: x=2, y=f(f(5))=f(26 mod25=1)=2, gcd(|2-2|,25)=gcd(0,25)=25 (trivial). Restart with c=3: x=y=2, f(x)=x²+3. Iteration 1: x=7, y=f(f(2))=f(7)=52 mod25=2, gcd(|7-2|,25)=gcd(5,25)=5 (non-trivial factor). Step 4: Recursively factor 5 and 25/5=5. Both are prime by Miller-Rabin. Step 5: Combine: factors=2^2 * 5^2.

Step 1: Trial division up to 123456789^(1/3)≈498, check primes up to 498: find 3, 123456789%3=0, factor=3, exponent=2 (123456789/3=41152263, 41152263/3=13717421), remaining=13717421. No other small factors. Step 2: Miller-Rabin on 13717421: composite. Step 3: Pollard's Rho on 13717421: x=y=2, c=1, f(x)=x²+1. After several iterations, find factor d=3607 (example trace: x=5, y=26, gcd=1; x=26, y=677, gcd=1; ... until x=1234, y=5678, gcd=3607). Step 4: Recursively factor 3607 and 13717421/3607=3803. Miller-Rabin shows both are prime. Step 5: Combine: factors=3^2 * 3607 * 3803.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard's Rho Factor Detection
ladders.desc: Implement Pollard's Rho algorithm to find a single non-trivial factor of a composite number.
ladders.explanation: Pollard's Rho algorithm is a probabilistic method to find a non-trivial factor d of a composite number n. The intuition comes from the birthday paradox and cycle detection: if we generate a pseudo-random sequence {x_k} modulo n, and if n has a factor d, then the sequence modulo d will repeat (cycle) more quickly than modulo n. By computing gcd(|x_i - x_j|, n) for different i,j, we can detect d when the gcd is neither 1 nor n. Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm is used efficiently with two pointers (x and y) where y moves twice as fast as x. The algorithm: 1) If n is even, return 2. 2) Initialize x=2, y=2, d=1, choose a random constant c (typically 1 ≤ c ≤ n-1, c≠0,-2). 3) Define f(x) = (x² + c) mod n (a polynomial function). 4) While d == 1: update x = f(x), y = f(f(y)), compute d = gcd(|x-y|, n). 5) If d == n, the algorithm failed; restart with different c. Otherwise, return d. Time complexity: O(n^(1/4)) expected due to the birthday paradox, as we need about O(√p) iterations to find a factor p of n. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This sub-routine is the core of the factorization process, called recursively to break down composite numbers. Common mistakes: using c=0 or c=-2 (which can cause short cycles), not handling the case when d==n (need to restart), and not using absolute value in gcd. Real code pattern: def pollard_rho(n): if n%2==0: return 2; x=y=2; d=1; c=random.randint(1,n-1); f=lambda x:(x*x+c)%n; while d==1: x=f(x); y=f(f(y)); d=math.gcd(abs(x-y),n); return d if d!=n else None.

examples.trace: Step 1: n is even, so immediately return 2 without running Rho algorithm.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection with Floyd's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Use Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm to detect cycles in a sequence modulo n.
ladders.explanation: Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm (also called tortoise and hare) detects cycles in a sequence by using two pointers that move at different speeds. The intuition is that if a sequence repeats (has a cycle), the faster pointer will eventually meet the slower pointer within the cycle. For Pollard's Rho, we generate a sequence x_{k+1} = f(x_k) mod n, where f is a pseudo-random function (like f(x)=x²+c). We initialize two values: x (tortoise) and y (hare). In each step, x moves one step: x = f(x), while y moves two steps: y = f(f(y)). If there exists a cycle modulo some factor d of n, then |x-y| will be a multiple of d after some iterations, and gcd(|x-y|, n) may reveal d. The algorithm continues until gcd ≠ 1. Time complexity: O(√p) where p is the smallest prime factor of n, due to the birthday paradox. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the mechanism within Pollard's Rho to efficiently find collisions. Common mistakes: not updating y correctly as f(f(y)), or stopping too early. Real code pattern: while True: x = f(x); y = f(f(y)); d = gcd(abs(x-y), n); if d > 1: break.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize x=2, y=2. Step 2: Iteration 1: x=f(2)=5, y=f(f(2))=f(5)=26 mod15=11, gcd(|5-11|,15)=gcd(6,15)=3. Since 3>1, break. Found factor 3.

Step 1: x=1, y=1. Step 2: Iteration 1: x=f(1)=3, y=f(f(1))=f(3)=11, gcd(|3-11|,21)=gcd(8,21)=1. Step 3: Iteration 2: x=f(3)=11, y=f(f(11))=f(123 mod21=18)=326 mod21=326-21*15=326-315=11, gcd(|11-11|,21)=gcd(0,21)=21 (trivial). Restart with different c if needed.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic and GCD Computation
ladders.desc: Compute greatest common divisor (GCD) using Euclidean algorithm and perform modular arithmetic operations.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic involves operations on integers modulo n, where results are reduced to the range 0 to n-1. The GCD of two numbers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both. The Euclidean algorithm computes GCD efficiently: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) until b=0. For Pollard's Rho, we need to compute gcd(|x-y|, n) where x and y are values from the sequence modulo n. The intuition is that if |x-y| shares a common factor with n, then gcd will reveal that factor. Steps: 1) Compute diff = |x-y|. 2) Use Euclidean algorithm: while b≠0: a,b = b, a%b. Start with a=diff, b=n. Time complexity: O(log min(a,b)) for GCD. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: GCD is used to detect when |x-y| and n have a non-trivial common factor, indicating a cycle modulo a factor of n. Common mistakes: not taking absolute value (which can lead to negative numbers affecting mod), or not handling large numbers efficiently (use built-in gcd). Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a,b = b, a%b; return abs(a). For modular arithmetic: (x*y) mod n = ((x mod n)*(y mod n)) mod n.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=21, b=15. Step 2: Since b≠0, compute a,b = 15, 21%15=6. Now a=15, b=6. Step 3: b≠0, compute a,b = 6, 15%6=3. Now a=6, b=3. Step 4: b≠0, compute a,b = 3, 6%3=0. Now a=3, b=0. Step 5: b=0, so gcd=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pseudo-random Sequence Generation
ladders.desc: Generate a sequence using a recurrence relation modulo n, such as x_{k+1} = (x_k² + c) mod n.
ladders.explanation: A pseudo-random sequence is generated by a recurrence relation to simulate randomness. In Pollard's Rho, we use f(x) = (x² + c) mod n, where c is a constant (typically chosen randomly between 1 and n-1, avoiding 0 and -2). Starting from an initial value x0 (often 2), we compute x1 = f(x0), x2 = f(x1), etc. This creates a sequence that appears random but is deterministic. The intuition is that modulo a factor d of n, this sequence will eventually repeat (cycle) due to the pigeonhole principle, and the cycle length is expected to be O(√d). Steps: 1) Choose c. 2) Define f(x). 3) Initialize x = x0. 4) Iterate: x = f(x). Time complexity: O(1) per iteration. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This sequence is used in cycle detection; the values are fed into GCD computations. Common mistakes: using c=0 (leads to fixed points) or c=-2 (can cause short cycles), or not reducing modulo n properly. Real code pattern: c = random.randint(1, n-1); f = lambda x: (x*x + c) % n; x = 2; for _ in range(k): x = f(x).

examples.trace: Step 1: f(x)=x²+3 mod 10. Step 2: x0=1. Step 3: x1=1²+3=4 mod10=4. Step 4: x2=4²+3=19 mod10=9. Step 5: x3=9²+3=84 mod10=4. Step 6: x4=4²+3=19 mod10=9. Sequence: 1,4,9,4,9.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factorization
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, composite numbers, and the basic idea of breaking a number into prime factors.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself (e.g., 2,3,5,7). A composite number has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself (e.g., 4=2×2, 6=2×3). Prime factorization is the process of expressing a composite number as a product of prime numbers raised to exponents, unique by the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. For example, 12 = 2² × 3. The intuition is that any integer can be broken down uniquely into primes, which are the building blocks of numbers. To factor a number n: 1) Check if n is prime (if yes, output n). 2) Find a divisor d of n (1 < d < n). 3) Recursively factor d and n/d. Basic method: trial division by primes up to √n. Time complexity: O(√n). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This foundational concept motivates the need for efficient algorithms like Pollard's Rho for large numbers. Common mistakes: confusing prime with composite, or not handling exponents correctly. Real code pattern: def factor(n): i=2; while i*i<=n: if n%i==0: output i; n//=i; else: i+=1; if n>1: output n.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=12. Step 2: Check divisor 2: 12%2=0, so factor 2, n=12/2=6. Step 3: Again check 2: 6%2=0, factor 2, n=6/2=3. Step 4: Check 2: 3%2≠0. Step 5: Check 3: 3%3=0, factor 3, n=3/3=1. Step 6: n=1, stop. Factors: 2 appears twice, 3 once → 2^2 * 3.

Step 1: n=30. Step 2: Check 2: 30%2=0, factor 2, n=15. Step 3: Check 2: 15%2≠0. Step 4: Check 3: 15%3=0, factor 3, n=5. Step 5: Check 3: 5%3≠0. Step 6: Check 4: not prime, skip. Step 7: Check 5: 5%5=0, factor 5, n=1. Factors: 2,3,5.
